coredata.dc:title: High-speed imaging of ultrasound driven cavitation bubbles in blind and through holes
coredata.dc:creator: Kauer, Markus
Belova-Magri, Valentina
Cairós, Carlos
Linka, Gerd
Mettin, Robert
coredata.dc:description: The interest in application of ultrasonic cavitation for cleaning and surface treatment processes has increased greatly in the last decades. However, not much is known about the behavior of cavitation bubbles inside the microstructural features of the solid substrates. Here we report on an experimental study on dynamics of acoustically driven (38.5 kHz) cavitation bubbles inside the blind and through holes of PMMA plates by using high-speed imaging. Various diameters of blind (150, 200, 250 and 1000 µm) and through holes (200 and 1000 µm) were investigated. Gas bubbles are usually trapped in the holes during substrate immersion in the liquid thus preventing their complete wetting. We demonstrate that trapped gas can be successfully removed from the holes under ultrasound agitation. Besides the primary Bjerknes force and acoustic streaming, the shape oscillations of the trapped gas bubble seem to be a driving force for bubble removal out of the holes. We further discuss the bubble dynamics inside microholes for water and Cu2+ salt solution. It is found that the hole diameter and partly the type of liquid media influences the number, size and dynamics of the cavitation bubbles. The experiments also showed that a large amount of the liquid volume inside the holes can be displaced within one acoustic cycle by the expansion of the cavitation bubbles. This confirmed that ultrasound is a very effective tool to intensify liquid exchange processes, and it might significantly improve micro mixing in small structures. The investigation of the effect of ultrasound power on the bubble density distribution revealed the possibility to control the cavitation bubble distribution inside the microholes. At a high ultrasound power (31.5 W) we observed the highest bubble density at the hole entrances, while reducing the ultrasound power by a factor of ten shifted the bubble locations to the inner end of the blind holes or to the middle of the through holes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spatial bubbles distribution
Microholes
High speed imaging
Wetting
Bubble dynamics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasound assisted wetting of blind and through holes
Effect of ultrasound power on the cavitation bubble density distribution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Foundation for Research, Technology and Development
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Foundation for Science and Technology Development
Austrian Federal Ministry of Economy, Family and Youth

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank Atotech Deutschland GmbH for financial support and the members of the mechanical workshop at DPI for valuable support. R. M. and C. C. acknowledge financial support by the Austrian Federal Ministry of Economy, Family and Youth and the National Foundation for Research, Technology and Development.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plot of calorimetric power and acoustic pressure measured at a distance of 11 cm to the transducer surface for different power settings of the ultrasound generator.

Finite Element Method Simulation of the acoustic pressure distribution in the 3d sonoreactor (Comsol Multiphysics 5.0 [21]; colour plot of the acoustic pressure in the central vertical plane, time near the maximum pressure excursion; linear model with arbitrary pressure units and 38.5 kHz excitation via the bottom spherical plate). The result shows a non-trivial standing wave pattern with the hole in the plate (arrow) being located slightly above a pressure antinode.

Selected frames from a high-speed recording (1 µs exposure time) showing the wetting process during ultrasound agitation in (a) 1 mm-wide and 1.2 mm-length blind hole, (b) 200 µm-wide and 1.2 mm-length blind hole and (c) 200 µm-wide and 2 mm-length through hole. The corresponding Movie S1 is available in the Supplementary material.

(a), (d), (e) Consecutive frames from high-speed recordings (150 kfps, 1 µs exposure time) of a 150 µm-wide and 1.2 mm-length blind hole filled with water; the left scale shows the period T of the ultrasound wave. The corresponding Movie S2 is available in the Supplementary material. (b) The dynamics of the bubble length d h (horizontal expansion) for different bubbles position in the blind hole. The numbers of the curves correlate to the numbered bubbles in (a) at 33.3 µs. (c) Experimental results for the bubble lifetime as a function of the distance to the inner end of the blind hole.

(a) Consecutive frames from a high-speed recordings (186 kfps, 1.7 µs exposure time) of 150 µm-wide and 1.2 mm-length blind hole filled with salt solution; the left scale shows the period T of the ultrasound wave. The corresponding Movie S2 is available in the supplementary material. (b) The dynamics of the bubble length d h (horizontal expansion) for different bubbles position in the blind hole. The numbers of the curves correlate with the numbered bubbles in (a) at 21.5 µs. (c) Experimental results for the bubble lifetime as a function of the distance to the inner end of the blind hole.

(a) Snapshot and (b), (c) consecutive frames from a video sequence (300 kfps, 1 µs exposure time) of the 200 µm-wide and 2 mm-length through hole in water; (b) shows the bubble collapse in the center of the hole for the neutral collapse and (c) of the bubble collapse attached at the wall. The corresponding videos are available in Movie S3 (see Supplementary material). (d) Experimental results for the bubble length d h (horizontal expansion) as a function of time. Data of (b) and (c) correspond to times from 0 µs to 117 µs and 103 µs.

(a) Consecutive frames from a video sequence (150 kfps, 2.3 µs exposure time) in a 200 µm-wide and 2 mm-length through hole filled with salt solution; the left scale shows the period T of the ultrasound wave. The white dotted circles mark bubbles which apparently show jetting. The corresponding Movie S3 is available in the Supplementary material. (b) Experimental results for the bubble lifetimes as a function of position in the through hole.

Snapshots (left, exposure time: 1 µs for water and 2.3 µs for salt solution) and spatial bubble density distribution images (right) for various 1.2 mm-length blind hole diameters (150, 200, 250 and 1000 µm). Experiments were carried out in water and in salt solution. The white dotted lines mark the hole dimension.

Spatial bubble density distribution for various through hole diameters (200 and 1000 µm) and 2 mm-length. Experiments were carried out in water and salt solution. The white dotted lines mark the hole dimension.

Effect of ultrasound power on spatial bubble density distribution in 2 mm-length through ((b) 200 µm-wide and (d) 1 mm-wide) and 1.2 mm-length blind holes ((a) 1 mm-wide and (c) 150 µm-wide). Experiments were carried out in water (a) and in salt solution (b–d). The white dotted lines mark the hole dimension.

head.ce:title: High-speed imaging of ultrasound driven cavitation bubbles in blind and through holes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Atotech Deutschland GmbH, Erasmusstraße 20, 10553 Berlin, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drittes Physikalisches Institut, Georg-August-Universität Göttingen, Friedrich-Hund-Platz 1, 37077 Göttingen, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interest in application of ultrasonic cavitation for cleaning and surface treatment processes has increased greatly in the last decades. However, not much is known about the behavior of cavitation bubbles inside the microstructural features of the solid substrates. Here we report on an experimental study on dynamics of acoustically driven (38.5 kHz) cavitation bubbles inside the blind and through holes of PMMA plates by using high-speed imaging. Various diameters of blind (150, 200, 250 and 1000 µm) and through holes (200 and 1000 µm) were investigated. Gas bubbles are usually trapped in the holes during substrate immersion in the liquid thus preventing their complete wetting. We demonstrate that trapped gas can be successfully removed from the holes under ultrasound agitation. Besides the primary Bjerknes force and acoustic streaming, the shape oscillations of the trapped gas bubble seem to be a driving force for bubble removal out of the holes. We further discuss the bubble dynamics inside microholes for water and Cu2+ salt solution. It is found that the hole diameter and partly the type of liquid media influences the number, size and dynamics of the cavitation bubbles. The experiments also showed that a large amount of the liquid volume inside the holes can be displaced within one acoustic cycle by the expansion of the cavitation bubbles. This confirmed that ultrasound is a very effective tool to intensify liquid exchange processes, and it might significantly improve micro mixing in small structures. The investigation of the effect of ultrasound power on the bubble density distribution revealed the possibility to control the cavitation bubble distribution inside the microholes. At a high ultrasound power (31.5 W) we observed the highest bubble density at the hole entrances, while reducing the ultrasound power by a factor of ten shifted the bubble locations to the inner end of the blind holes or to the middle of the through holes.

ce:section.ce:para: The dynamics of acoustically driven bubbles in bulk liquid as well as near a rigid wall have been extensively studied in the literature – [1,2]. The rapid development of microfluidic devices, in particular for lab-on-a-chip-applications, increases the interest in bubbles dynamics in microholes and microchannels. This is particularly important for cleaning processes and treatment of surfaces with sophisticated geometry (e.g. conformal metal deposition). Oscillating bubbles can act as micropumps [3] and microfluidic mixers inducing the disruption of laminar flow in microchannels and thus providing a flow with a higher Reynolds number [4–6].

There are several previous studies in the literature on dynamic behavior of laser-induced bubbles [3,7–10] and spark-generated bubbles [11,12] in microchannels or narrow gaps filled with different fluids. By using focused laser pulses or sparks a controllable cavitation bubble can be generated and observed through a transparent cell using high-speed imaging. For example, Dijkink et al. [3] studied a micropump driven by a single controlled laser-induced cavitation bubble. They have shown that picoliter volumes of fluid can be pumped through the microchannel due to the jetting phenomenon. However, so far, only very few studies on the bubble dynamics behavior driven by ultrasound in small channels or holes have been carried out [4,6,13,14]. For example, Ozcelik et al. [6] investigated the mixing ability of a multibubble system driven at an ultrasound frequency of 120 kHz in a PDMS microchannel. The bubbles reached a size only up to 20 µm, whereby the imaging of the bubble dynamics was very difficult to perform.

The aim of this work is to gain insight into the dynamical behavior of acoustic cavitation bubbles in blind and through holes at an acoustic frequency of 38.5 kHz, where the bubble size is expected to be large enough to apply high-speed imaging. The investigations were carried out with optically transparent PMMA plates. The plates have a mechanically drilled hole from 150 μm to 1000 µm in diameter. Two different liquid media were used for the experiments: DI water and aqueous Cu2+ salt solution. During the immersion of the plate in the liquid, typically air is trapped in the holes, thus preventing full wetting of the plate. First, we will discuss in detail the removal of the trapped air by ultrasound agitation which ensures a complete wetting of the holes in a short time-interval. Then, we will demonstrate that cavitation bubbles appear in particular inside the holes, and their dynamics is discussed. We found that the type of liquid media and the hole diameter influence the number of cavitation bubbles, their size, distribution and dynamics in the holes. Finally we observe that the distribution of bubbles in the hole can be successfully influenced by the ultrasound power. The possibility of spatial control of cavitation in microholes shows a great potential to improve cleaning processes, surface treatments, or sonochemistry applications.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic drawing of the experimental setup for the generation and observation of cavitation processes. All experiments were performed in a fused quartz rectangular sonoreactor (60 × 60 × 170 mm3) filled with 500 ml liquid. The sonoreactor is fitted with two lateral cooling jackets, which are connected with a thermostat (Eco Silver, RE415S, Lauda, Germany). The cooling jackets were filled with a thermal liquid (0.1 g/l Na2CO3 in DI water (max. conductivity 0.0005 mS/cm)). The liquid temperature inside the reactor was controlled with a thermocouple (Pt 100-94, Lauda, Germany) and maintained at a constant temperature of 25 °C for all experiments. The sonoreactor was connected at the bottom with a transducer (Meinhardt, Germany) driven by an ultrasound generator (SONOREX TG 50-S, BANDELIN, Germany). The transducer is driven at around 38.5 kHz. It should be noted that the pressure amplitude is modulated with 100 Hz through unfiltered rectified 50 Hz AC voltage from the power supply. This is typical for commercial high-power ultrasonic systems and could not be changed. However, most high-speed recordings of cavitation bubbles are short compared to the modulation period, thus momentarily constant driving amplitude is assumed.

Ultrasonic transmitted power was determined calorimetrically using the method as described earlier in [15,16], and the acoustic pressure was measured with a hydrophone (HCT-0300, Onda, USA) connected to an acoustic meter (MCT-1010, Onda, USA) which shows the time-averaged pressure amplitude value (here over 10 s). The hydrophone was positioned 11 cm above the transducer surface at the PMMA plate surface (see Section 2.2) close to the hole. Fig. 2 shows the calorimetric power and the acoustic pressure for five available power settings (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100%) of the ultrasound generator. A relatively linear increase from 3.3 W to 31.5 W is observed for the calorimetric power. The acoustic pressure increased linear at a power setting from 20 to 60%. Above the power settings of 60% the acoustic pressure increased only slightly and saturates (225 kPa). Possibly a high number of cavitation bubbles near the transducer surface leads to a shielding of the sound wave [17–19]. In the following discussions, we usually refer to calorimetric power.

Two types of liquid medium were used in this study: (i) DI water and (ii) aqueous salt solution (2.5 M H2SO4, 0.35 M CuSO4 and different additives in low concentration) which is typically used in copper plating processes. The salt solution has a lower surface tension (39.7 mN/m) and a higher density (1.20 g/l) than water (72.0 mN/m, 100 g/l [20]). The dynamic viscosity for water and salt solution is 1.0 and 3.5 mPa s, respectively. The substrates are transparent PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate) plates with dimensions of 60 × 10 mm2 and a thickness of 2 mm. The through or blind holes of various sizes were mechanically drilled in the center of the plates. Investigated through holes were with a diameter of 200 and 1000 µm and blind holes with depth of 1.2 mm and diameters of 150, 200, 250 and 1000 µm. In the following we use the expression microholes for all geometries. In the experiments the plates were immersed into the sonoreactor filled with liquid medium and placed vertically above the transducer surface such that the height of the hole amounted to 11 cm. The hole/transducer-distance was selected due to technical reasons: Only this way a suitable illumination could be reached and subsequent plating experiments have been possible (not reported here). In the indicated position, significant acoustic cavitation at and inside the hole could be produced, consistently with the pressure amplitude up to 225 kPa (Fig. 2). Additionally, computations of the sound field distribution in the cuvette have been performed with a commercial Finite Element code (Comsol Multiphysics [21]). They are based on linear wave propagation (neglecting the influence of the cavitation bubbles which is considered to be small, compare [16]) with suitable boundary conditions (sound soft on top and impedance conditions at the glass walls and the substrate). The results show a pressure antinode slightly below the hole position, see Fig. 3 . This result is also consistent with the experimental observations.

Observations of bubble dynamics were performed using a high-speed camera (FASTCAM SA5, Photron, Japan) fitted with a long distance microscope (K2/SC, INFINITY, USA) and backlight illumination (LS-352A, Sumita, Japan). The bubble dynamics were recorded from the side view of the holes (see Fig. 1). The bubbles appear dark in the high-speed videos due to deflection at the liquid/bubble interface. To enhance brightness and contrast of the videos, the recorded images were processed using the software ImageJ (National Institutes of Health, USA) [22].

Temporally averaged images of spatial bubble distribution were generated with ImageJ for all recorded videos. First, a background image, where no bubbles are visible, was subtracted from the video frames in order to get a frame sequence, where only cavitation bubbles are visible. Next, an average intensity image was calculated from the sequence and transformed into a 16 false color image after brightness and contrast were adjusted. The average images show the temporally averaged spatial bubble distribution, where the bubble density increases with the following color sequence: blue, green, yellow, red, white. The areas where no bubbles were present or detected are black.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasound assisted wetting of blind and through holes
ce:section.ce:para:
First, we started with investigations of wetting processes in through and blind microholes in the applied ultrasound field at a power of 31.5 W. The following experiments were carried out in both liquid media. Fig. 4 a shows nine images from a representative sequence describing the wetting process of a blind hole of 1 mm diameter, where the dynamic behavior of a trapped air bubble can be observed (see Supplementary material, Movie S1). The elapsed time for each frame is calculated from the start of the ultrasound and given in milliseconds in the right upper corner. The frame at 0.00 ms shows the initial condition where the hole is initially full of air, which is trapped during the immersion of the plate in water. In the Movie S1 at time of about 20 ms we observe first shape oscillations at the surface of the trapped air bubble. We could not, however, notice a full oscillation of the air bubble (the bubble volume does not change), since the air bubble radius (>500 µm) is significantly larger than the resonance bubble radius R r at the applied ultrasound frequency, which can be calculated by R r ≈ 3 m s−1/f [23,24]. For ultrasound frequency of f = 38.5 kHz we obtain a resonance radius of about 78 µm. Surface instabilities at the gas bubble/liquid-interface form capillary waves. We assume that small water droplets are ejected into the bubble due to a high amplitude of the capillary wave. This process was reported and imaged by Tandiono et al. [25] in a microchannel. It is the same effect which occurs in ultrasonic atomization, where small liquid droplets eject from the surface at high frequencies by capillary waves (compare with [26,27]). Here, however, the droplets are ejected into the bubble interior and fill it up with liquid. In the Movie S1 droplets are observable which move from the entrance to the inner end of the blind hole. The water droplets are marked by a white dotted outline in the frame at 46.48 ms. The drops start to accumulate in the upper right part of the hole which is marked with an arrow (85.82 ms). At 205.80 ms a similar volume of water is observed at the lower right corner of the hole where the contact line between trapped air bubble and the inner end of the hole is drastically decreased. The increased water volume at the inner end pushes the air bubble out of the hole. At 237.02 ms a big part of the air bubble is outside of the hole. The next frame (333.76 ms) shows that the air bubble continues to move out of the hole. The outer part of the bubble is directed upwards along the PMMA plate/liquid interface. Possible reasons for this bending effect could be the buoyancy force, ultrasound induced streaming, or the primary Bjerknes force, since in an acoustic standing wave field, large gas bubbles are pushed to the pressure nodes by the primary Bjerknes force. The latter explanation would be consistent with the numerical results shown in Fig. 3, since a pressure antinode slightly below the hole position would cause an upwards force on the large bubble at the hole entrance. In the frame at 338.66 ms the gas bubble moves slightly back into the hole and the shape-oscillations appear significantly weaker than that of the previous frame. This phenomenon can be observed each 10 ms in the video sequence. As we discussed before, the pressure amplitude is modulated with 100 Hz, thus the acoustic pressure has every 10 ms a minimum which is reflected in a weaker shape oscillation. The simultaneous back motion of the bubble speaks as well in favour of the Bjerknes force as pulling agent, since buoyancy should not depend strongly on the driving, and acoustic streaming should act with inertia large enough to flatten the modulation. Finally, after 353.22 ms the air bubble detaches from the blind hole and moves upwards to the water surface leaving the hole completely wetted.
Movie S1
Fig. 4b shows five sequential frames illustrating the wetting process for a 200 µm-wide blind hole. Here, the shape oscillations/capillary waves of trapped air bubbles at the hole entrance could not be observed, possibly due to the resolution limit of the high-speed camera and light scattering/diffraction at the circular hole profile. We assume similar shape oscillations at the surface of the gas bubble to what was detected in the 1 mm-wide blind hole, as we can also observe how water enters the blind hole at 6.75 ms. This can be observed in more detail in the Movie S1 provided in the supplementary material. The main part of the blind hole is rapidly filled with water after 7.25 ms. At this stage, only three air bubbles are still detected inside the hole. Their radius is around 75 µm, which is equivalent to the calculated resonance radius (R r ≈ 78 µm). In Movie S1 surface mode oscillations of the bubbles are observable. The air bubbles split into smaller ones, which can also be observed in the presented frame at 605.25 ms. The frame rate of the video (4000 fps) is too low to observe the bubble splitting process. However, we assume that strong bubble oscillations break up the gas bubbles. The pressure gradient of the standing acoustic wave seems to push newly formed small bubbles out of the hole to the pressure nodes (primary Bjerknes force). After 660.75 ms, only one oscillating bubble remains in the hole. The observed wetting process in 200 μm-wide blind holes is comparable with 150 and 250 µm-wide holes.

The frame sequence in Fig. 4c depicts the situation, where the fast progress of the wetting process in the 200 µm-wide through hole is similar to the 200 µm-wide blind hole. After only 12.60 ms, the main part of the trapped air is removed. One elongated bubble of approximately 530 µm length stays in the central part and one smaller round-shaped bubble (r = 80 µm) in the left part of the through hole, where the acoustic pressure is probably lower than at both hole entrances. At both sides of the big air bubble (at the liquid/air interface) we can observe shape oscillations which lead to splitting of small gas bubbles (see Movie S1 in the Supplementary material 12.60–436.24 ms). They move to the hole entrance and drift away from the hole. At 436.24 ms the big air bubble which has remained in the center of the hole shrinks to 390 µm length. Then, the air bubble travels to the left entrance (440.16 ms). A possible driving force for the bubble movement could be the sonocapillary effect [28]. The presence of cavitation bubbles at the right entrance increases the pressure inside the hole which pushes the gas bubble to the left hole entrance, where it splits, resulting in smaller bubbles (440.44 ms) which move predominantly out of the hole. After 476 ms of elapsed time the through hole is mostly wetted.

In all discussed above cases, when ultrasound is applied, the trapped air bubble is removed in less than 1 s. Driving forces for the ultrasound induced removal of the trapped air bubbles are shape oscillations with internal atomization, ultrasound induced streaming, the sonocapillary effect, and the primary Bjerknes force. All effects can also work together. For applications that desire uniform wetting at every location of a larger surface, motion or oscillation of the substrate might be necessary to reach this goal in an acoustic standing wave field.

We now focus on the dynamical behavior of the cavitation bubbles inside the blind microholes filled with water at ultrasound power of 31.5 W. Fig. 5 a, d, e shows a sequence of frames for a 150 µm-wide blind hole. The Movie S2 is presented in the Supplementary material. In general, we find three different types of cavitation bubble behavior in the blind hole which we categorize as “plugged bubble chain”, “stuck end bubble” and “dispersed small bubbles”. All three types of cavitation bubble behavior apparently occur randomly and are not correlated to acoustic pressure or other applied parameters.
Movie S2
Fig. 5a shows the first type such as “plugged bubbles chain”, where a row of cavitation bubbles is arranged next to each other along the hole. The expanded bubble diameters reach about channel width, which causes a slug flow situation. The collapses of the bubbles advance in a stepwise manner starting from the entrance of the blind hole to its inner end. In the frame at 33.3 µs the bubbles are numbered sequentially from right to left (1 to 7). The dynamics of the labeled bubbles is illustrated in Fig. 5b. For the plot we used the horizontal length of the bubble d h instead of its radius, due to the fact that these cavitation bubbles are restrained by the hole walls. They partly expand along the channel which leads to an elongated shape. The expansion of the bubbles in the hole starts simultaneously as can be seen in the frames at 0 µs and 6.7 µs, and we define their lifetime as the time interval between start of growth and collapse peak. The bubbles expand and some of them merge with adjacent small bubbles during this process (e.g. bubble 3 or 4 at 26.7 µs). The bubbles 7, 6 and 5 collapse first, which happens after about two acoustic cycles at 53.3 µs (one cycle is around 26 µs). Bubbles 3 and 4 follow slightly before the frame at 60.0 µs. Although bubble 2 showed the smallest maximum size of the ensemble, its collapse is delayed until shortly after 60 µs. Finally bubble 1 at the inner end of the hole implodes at 73.3 µs. The lifetime of bubble 1 is nearly three times higher than one acoustic cycle, and this bubble reaches the highest horizontal bubble length (d h = 174 µm) in the hole compared to the other bubbles. In contrast to the bubbles inside the hole, the oscillation period of the cavitation bubbles at the entrance (outside the hole) corresponds to one acoustic cycle. Thus we see a remarkable delay time of the collapse from the entrance to the inner end of the blind hole (distance of 1.2 mm) of 30 µs. This is much less than the time needed for an acoustic pressure wave for the same distance (0.8 µs assuming a speed of sound of 1494 m/s [29]), indicating another mechanism than sound travel time. For a single bubble inside a tube or channel, it is known that the constriction of the liquid flow (due to the presence of the walls) can significantly reduce the bubble’s natural oscillation frequency (eigenfrequency of the volume mode), in particular if the bubble size reaches the channel dimensions [30]. The effect is mainly caused by an enlarged liquid inertia term [31], but also additional friction at the solid boundaries plays a role [32]. Thus the oscillation of strongly expanded bubbles in the hole might not be able to follow the acoustic driving, and collapses are “left out” (a mechanism that can lead to period doubling also in unbounded liquid [24]). Furthermore, the driving pressure field might be shielded by cavitation bubbles at the hole entrance and/or other expanded bubbles in the “plugged chain” along the hole. The peculiar avalanche collapse of such a chain towards the inner end, as shown in Fig. 5a, might be caused by subsequent shock wave triggers: The shock from the outer bubble collapse initiates the collapse of the inner neighbor bubble. If so, the bubble collapse time is the speed-limiting step for this mechanism and responsible for the delayed collapse of the rear bubble. Sankin et al. [33] and Klaseboer et al. [34] investigated shock triggered bubble collapses. They found collapsing times of 1–4 µs for a laser induced cavitation bubble with R max of 300 µm in an unbounded liquid domain and shock wave peak pressures of 39 MPa. From the radius-time traces in Fig. 5b we obtain collapse times of individual bubbles within the chain of the order of 5 µs. This slightly higher value could be consistent with the shock wave trigger assumption if one takes into account additional inertia of the bubbles due to the hole walls, and a smaller shock pressure peak. However, a complete quantitative discussion of the shock triggered bubble chain collapse in a blind hole is complicated and not further considered here. Nevertheless, it should be noted that an avalanche collapse might cause considerable mechanical loads on the hole walls, in particular on the inner ending.

The second type of cavitation bubbles behavior, the “stuck end bubble”, is shown in Fig. 5d. A single isolated large bubble is located at the inner end of the blind hole (highlighted with a black dotted outline). Here, this bubble grows over 46.7 µs to the hole diameter size (a maximum detected bubble length of 198 µm). The subsequent bubble collapse leads to the immediate formation of an adjacent bubble cluster at 53.3 µs (highlighted with a white dotted outline). The lifetime of the bubble located at the inner end as well as the bubble cluster are similar and approximately equal to two acoustic cycles. These extended lifetimes might again be caused by skipped collapses due to additional bubble inertia in the hole, or/and by shielding by bubbles in front of the hole entrance. We assume that the bubble cluster formation is triggered by a shock-wave resulting from the collapse of the bubble at the inner end of the blind hole. Later, the end bubble can re-expand.

Fig. 5e depicts the third observed type of cavitation bubble behavior in the 150 µm-wide blind hole: “dispersed small bubbles”. In contrast to type one, all bubbles collapse simultaneously after about two acoustic cycles. Here, the number and size of cavitation bubbles inside the hole as well as at the entrance are less than in the first case (Fig. 5a). Although the fewer entrance bubbles are possibly less efficient in shielding the rear bubbles from ultrasonic waves and/or shockwaves, again a retarded collapse in the hole is observed.

We found that in water the second type of cavitation bubble behavior in the 200 and 250 µm-wide blind holes is a dominant one, i.e. frequently collapse of a single bubble located at the inner end of the hole takes place. It is interesting to investigate how the bubble expansion is affected by the diameter of holes. The average vertical bubble diameter d v in the 150 µm-wide hole is about d v = 62 µm which is similar to the case with 200 µm-wide hole (d v = 63 µm) and this value increases slightly up to d v = 68 µm for the blind hole of 250 µm width. The investigations of cavitation bubble dynamics in 1 mm-wide through and blind holes were not carried out, as our preliminary study showed that no sufficient resolution was obtained at high frame rates (100 kfps) to observe details of the bubble dynamics.

During the bubble expansion liquid exits, while it enters the hole during the bubble collapse phase. Thus cavitation bubbles can indeed act as micropumps, providing an extremely fast liquid exchange and mixing inside the hole. This is beneficial for mass transfer limited reactions in the high aspect ratio (>1:8) microholes. To estimate the amount of liquid in the hole that can be displaced by the expansion of cavitation bubbles, we calculate total bubble volumes V B in the 150 µm-blind hole. The bubble volume is extracted by image processing assuming a generalized spherical bubble shape. The volume of the blind hole V H is about 21.2 pl. In Fig. 5e, 5.7% (V B = 1.2 pl) of the blind hole volume is filled with cavitation bubbles at 40 µs. For the stepwise collapse in Fig. 5a, the bubble volume is higher (V B = 4.2 pl) and 20% of the water volume in the hole is displaced at 40.0 µs. This would mean that 1/5 of the liquid in the hole can be exchanged in only one bubble cycle. A similar bubble volume of 3.5 pl (17% of the total hole volume) is estimated for the case where a single bubble is located at the inner end of the hole (Fig. 5d at 46.7 µs). Here, for volume calculation we assume a cylindrical bubble shape.

Next, we study the bubble dynamics in the blind hole of the same size as previously discussed (150 µm), but in the second liquid medium (salt solution of lower surface tension and higher viscosity than water). The results in the salt solution show mainly the first type of cavitation bubble behavior, where the chain of spherically-shaped cavitation bubbles is distributed along the blind hole (compare with Movie S2 in Supplementary material). Fig. 6 a illustrates a representative sequence of frames, where the bubbles are numbered from right to left at 21.5 µs. The bubble formation starts simultaneously, and the dynamics of the horizontal expansion for the numbered bubbles is shown in Fig. 6b. We measure at 43.0 µs the highest total bubble volume (spherical shapes are assumed) of about V b = 1.3 pl, corresponding to 6.1% of the total hole volume. Similar to water, a stepwise bubble collapse starts from the entrance of the hole to the inner end as shown in Fig. 6b. Bubble 5 is located at the highest distance to the inner end and has the shortest lifetime (37.7 µs, around 1.5 acoustic cycles). Bubble 1, however, shows to have a longer lifetime (70 µs, corresponding to 2.5 acoustic cycles). The delay time of the bubble collapse from the entrance to the end of the blind hole is around 55 µs and slightly significantly higher than that shown in Fig. 5a.

The delay time varies for every bubble cycle. To estimate the average delay time of the collapse, the bubble lifetime and distance of the bubble to the inner end of the hole were measured for 80 bubbles and summarized in Fig. 5c for water and in Fig. 6c for salt solution. The average lifetime for bubbles in water is about 57 µs, which is quite similar to salt solution (56 µs). The plots (Figs. 5c and 6c) do not reflect the spatial density distribution of the bubbles in the blind hole, which will be discussed in the next section. The slope of the linear fits provide an approximation of the delay time of the collapses along the entire hole length (1.2 mm). The averaged delay time is in salt solution slightly higher (22 µs) than in water (20 µs), but the results are essentially the same.

Similar behavior of cavitation bubbles (like in Fig. 6a) we observe in the blind holes with a diameter of 200 and 250 µm-wide and length of 1.2 mm in salt solution. On the contrary to the investigations in water, the diameter of holes has a stronger impact on vertical bubble expansion d v in salt solution. For example, in the 200 µm-wide hole, the average vertical bubble diameter is estimated to be about d v = 64 µm, whereas in the 250 µm-wide hole the vertical bubble diameter is higher and equal to 68 µm. For the 150 µm hole the vertical bubble diameter decreases to 51 µm due to space limitation by the small hole diameter. This effect is not so pronounced in pure water. Possibly the higher viscosity of the salt solution leads to a more effective damping of bubble expansion by the close walls. Furthermore, the acoustic pressure distribution might somehow be affected by the increased damping of the ultrasound wave due to the higher viscosity.

In the following we focus on the cavitation process inside a 200 µm-wide through hole in water. Fig. 7 a displays data from a typical recording (at 31.5 W). Cavitation occurs at the PMMA plate surface and at both entrances of the through hole. A remarkable feature is a larger bubble trapped at the center (i.e. at about half length of the hole; see Movie S3 in the Supplementary material). It appears immediately after turning the ultrasound on, and after turning off a remnant bubble of about 40 µm radius remains in the hole center. The remnant bubble size is significantly smaller than the resonance radius (R r ≈ 78 µm), and if the trapping is due to primary Bjerknes forces [35] one might expect a sound pressure maximum near the center. However, in a sound field simulation inside the hole (using again Comsol [21] without bubbles present), we observe a slight decrease of acoustic pressure amplitude towards the center, not an increase. These results are only preliminary and not included here. In any case, closer analysis of the direction of primary Bjerknes forces requires taking into account the restricted geometry and the observed diameter-time dynamics (period-doubled, compare Fig. 7d), which is beyond the scope of this paper.
Movie S3
During the final stage of the bubble collapse, two different scenarios can be observed (compare with Movie S3 in Supplementary material). The first scenario is a “neutral” bubble collapse where the bubble appears to fill the full hole diameter, as shown in Fig. 7b. Here, the cavitation bubble is initially small. The shape of the bubble changes from spherical to a cylindrical one due to the limited space inside the hole (occupying the main part of the frame). The largest horizontal bubble diameter of d h = 253 µm is estimated from the frame 12 (t = 40 µs). This value corresponds to V B = 7.9 pl of a bubble volume, which is about 13% of the total volume of the hole (V H = 62.8 pl). During the collapse stage the bubble contracts in the horizontal direction. The collapse, however, seems to take place at the center of the hole (without any clearly visible jetting).

Fig. 7c shows the case with the second collapse scenario. Here, an asymmetrical bubble collapse occurs at the wall, presented in the frames 13–16 and 28–31. Frames 1–5 and 17–21 show the initial bubble growth before collapse with a maximum horizontal bubble length of d h = 207 µm. The hemispherical bubble is attached at the lower wall of the hole and does not reach the upper wall at the highest expansion in frame 5. During the collapse towards the wall no jetting is visible (but might be hidden).

The dynamics of the neutral collapse is compared with the bubble collapse attached at the wall of the hole in Fig. 7d in terms of bubble length vs. time. Both oscillations are period-doubled, i.e. the collapses repeat after about 2 driving periods. The periodicity is, however, not exact, and shorter cycles or afterbounces appear to restore synchronization with the driving.

In Fig. 8 a, the same experimental conditions are repeated as in Fig. 7, but in the second liquid medium (salt solution). The initial cavitation bubbles in the first frame (0 µs) appear simultaneously. In this case, more than one cavitation bubble is observed in the through hole, e.g. 25 mostly spherical cavitation bubbles are visible during bubble expansion in the frame at 19.98 µs. The maximum horizontal bubble length of d h = 125 µm is reached at 46.62 µs. The behavior of the cavitation bubbles during their collapse is comparable to the one with blind holes (Fig. 6a), where it was found that the bubbles do not collapse simultaneously. The collapse in Fig. 8a starts from both entrances of the through hole at 26.64 µs and continuous toward to its center, where bubbles collapse at 53.28 µs. In the Movie S3 (Supplementary material) asymmetric bubble collapses can be observed. The white dotted outlines in Fig. 8a, at 33.30 µs and 46.62 µs mark two bubbles with clearly non-spherical collapse. This fact allows us to assume the presence of jetting in such a system. The shape of the collapsing bubbles indicates that the jets are directed toward the center of the through hole and not toward the walls, otherwise a common cause of jetting [36,37]. Fig. 8b shows the experimentally measured bubble lifetimes as a function of the through hole length. With two linear fits from the entrances of the through hole to the central part an averaged delay time of 37 µs was calculated for the entrance-center distance. The maximum bubbles' volume of V B = 3.3 pl, which corresponds to 5% of the total hole volume, is obtained at 39.96 µs.

With the comparison of our above discussed results, it can be deduced that in all investigated through hole dimensions the cavitation bubbles are able to exchange liquid medium inside the holes. In case of 5% of the total liquid volume displacement in the through hole within only two acoustic cycles (52 µs), the liquid volume should be multiple times exchanged within 1 s. The fast liquid exchange provides an opportunity to use ultrasound driven cavitation bubbles as micromixer and micropumps in small holes.

In conclusion, the bubble dynamics in the through holes appears roughly like a “double blind hole”, i.e. left/right symmetric and each half similar to a single blind hole: a larger bubble forms at the center of the through hole (corresponding to the larger bubble at the inner end of a blind hole), and if many larger bubbles occur in a row (plug flow), their collapse is retarded towards the center (like towards the inner end for the blind hole). This type of symmetry between blind and through holes will be seen also in the following subsection.

To investigate the spatial cavitation bubble distribution inside the holes, long-term averaged images were created from high-speed videos (averaged over at least 100 frames, see Section 2.3 Imaging and evaluation). These images show the time-averaged bubble density inside the holes and partially at the PMMA plate surface. Because of diffraction at the liquid/PMMA plate interface, a uniform illumination of the holes from the side is hardly possible, and bubbles at the upper and lower walls of the hole could not be detected. Therefore, the data shown does not cover those regions, but the obtained results provide sufficient insight on the bubbles density distribution around the symmetry axis of the holes. The investigations were carried out at an applied ultrasound power of 31.5 W.

Fig. 9 displays on the left side snapshots from the high-speed video and on the right side the corresponding images with spatial bubble density distribution in blind holes with diameters of 150, 200, 250 and 1000 µm for both investigated liquid media. All images show a high bubble density at the entrance of the hole. These observations confirm the findings of Rivas et al. [38,39] and Zijlstra et al. [40]. They had shown that the cavitation activity on a surface is enhanced due to the presence of micropits which might act as nucleation sites for microbubbles. Here (Fig. 9), we also see the mean spatial bubble density inside the blind holes. It turns out that this distribution is inhomogeneous. In DI water, for example, the highest intensity of the bubble density distribution is observed at the inner end of the holes, especially for those with 200 and 250 µm widths, where mainly one oscillating bubble is present. The distribution of bubbles according to time-averaged images along the blind holes length is more uniform in the case of the 150 µm-wide hole. A local maximum of the bubble density at 900 µm distance from the inner end can be observed. As already discussed above, we observed three types of cavitation bubble behavior for the 150 µm-wide blind hole in water, which might contribute significantly to bubble density distribution increase at the central part of the hole. In case of the 1 mm-wide blind hole, a weak maximum of the bubble density distribution is detectable at the inner end. In contrast to DI water medium, the bubble distribution inside the blind hole in salt solution is more uniform with maximum intensity at the inner end for all investigated hole diameters. In case of the 1 mm-wide blind hole the maximum intensity at the inner end is more pronounced than in water. For smaller hole diameters (150, 200 and 250 µm) a second maximum of the bubbles density distribution at 750–900 µm distances from the inner end of the hole can be detected in salt solution.

The general picture is a high bubble activity at hole entrance and inner end. The bubble density in between is lower (with some maximum at about 1/3 of total depth for the smaller holes). The bubble activity in the salt solution is more distributed than in water, which might be related to the lower surface tension. In some cases, a modulated (striped) pattern of bubble density occurs, possibly corresponding to the layers of neighbored bubbles. The bubble density outside the holes is higher in the upper part than in the lower part, which corresponds to the upward escape of initially trapped air (see Section 3.1). The reason should be similar, probably acoustic forces.

The images with averaged spatial distribution of bubble density in 200 µm and 1 mm-wide through holes in both investigated liquid media are presented in Fig. 10 . Again we observe a high bubble population at the entrances of the holes. A maximum of the bubble density distribution is located for water at the center of the 1 mm-wide through hole. Like before, the bubble density distribution in the hole is more uniform in salt solution. As already discussed above, virtually always one oscillating bubble (compare with Fig. 7a) is visible in the central part of the 200 µm-width through hole for water. This is illustrated by an intense maximum of the bubble density distribution in the center of the through hole for water (Fig. 10). In contrary, the cavitation bubbles in salt solution are detected over the whole length of the through hole in Fig. 8a, which provides a more uniform bubble density distribution in Fig. 10. The symmetry mentioned above occurs again, i.e. the averaged bubble distribution in the through holes appears somehow like the added distribution of two blind holes of half length, one from left and one from right.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasound power on the cavitation bubble density distribution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows selected images of the bubble density distribution in blind and through holes for variations of ultrasound power. The presented experiments were carried out in water for the 1 mm-wide blind hole as shown in Fig. 11a. Figures 11c, b, d shows the 150 µm-wide blind hole and the through holes with diameters of 200 µm and 1 mm in salt solution. As can be seen, the power of ultrasound affects significantly the bubble density distribution at and in the holes. In case of the blind holes (Fig. 11a, c), with decreasing ultrasound power from 31.5 W to 16.5 W, the maximum of the bubble density occurs closer to the inner end of the hole. We observe similar behavior for all blind holes in water as well as in salt solution. The effect of the ultrasound power on the bubble density distribution was stronger for salt solution, since the highest bubble population in water is still detected at the inner end of the hole at the highest power of 31.5. The effect of ultrasound power on the bubble density distribution inside the 1 mm-wide blind hole in water is very pronounced as can be seen in Fig. 11a. Reduction of ultrasound power from 31.5 W to 16.7 W shifts the bubble maximum from the entrance of the blind hole to its center. At 10% of applied ultrasound power (3.3 W) the highest bubble densities can be detected at the inner end of the hole. The obtained bubble density distribution inside the 150 µm-wide blind hole in salt solution (Fig. 11c) supports these findings. At low applied ultrasound power of 3.3 W any bubble density is only detectable at the inner end of the hole.

Similar to the case with blind holes (a, c), for through holes the bubble population shifts from the entrance to the central part by reducing the ultrasound power. In the 200 µm-wide through hole (in salt solution, see Fig. 11 Fig. 11b) at applied ultrasound power of 16.7 W we observe higher intensity of bubble distribution at both entrances and one maximum in the center of the hole. Reduction of ultrasound power to 10% (3.3 W) leads to an absence of bubbles at both entrances of the through hole. A single oscillating bubble is located at the center (compare the discussion above), and it is surrounded by small cavitation bubbles like in the case with water at ultrasound power of 31.5 W (Fig. 7a). Fig. 11d shows the influence of the ultrasound power on the bubble density distribution in a 1 mm-wide through hole in salt solution. At 8.7 W of ultrasound power we found the highest bubbles density in the central part of the through hole. Also here, we could not observe bubbles at both hole entrances and we suppose that the cavitation bubbles might shift to the center of the through holes by lowering the ultrasound power.

In general, we found that a decrease in ultrasound power can shift the cavitation bubbles to the inner end of the blind holes. Expressed differently, the cavitation appears to set in first at the inner end (center) of the blind hole (through hole). At higher power, bubble activity is shifted towards the openings of the holes, and the bubble density at the inner end (center) decreases. Shielding effects could be responsible for this behavior: At high ultrasound powers the large number of cavitation bubbles at the hole entrance effectively absorbs and scatters the acoustic wave, and the acoustic driving pressure decreases in the hole resulting in a lower number of cavitation bubbles (compare with [19,41]). Additionally, the sonocapillary effect [28,42–44] might lead to an increase of the static pressure inside the holes at high ultrasound power due to the presence of cavitation bubbles at the entrance. The higher static pressure could raise the cavitation threshold and decrease the cavitation bubble population in the hole.

The presented results demonstrate that the spatial distribution of cavitation bubbles at and inside the holes can be shifted by varying the ultrasound power. This type of control, however, acts somehow counterintuitively: Raising power increases significantly the bubble activity at the orifices, but reduces bubble density inside the holes.

In this work, we have shown a deeper investigation of ultrasonically assisted wetting processes and of the dynamics of cavitation bubbles inside microholes. It is observed that during ultrasound irradiation at 38.5 kHz the trapped bubbles can be removed from the blind and through holes in less than 1 s. The driving forces that move the air bubble out of the holes can be described by bubble shape oscillations and capillary waves causing “internal atomization”. Further, the primary Bjerknes force and potentially ultrasound induced acoustic streaming support the wetting by transporting the exterior part of the gas.

A correspondence was observed between the diameter of investigated holes, liquid medium and the bubble dynamics. The size of the expanded cavitation bubbles increases to some extent with increasing the hole diameter. Furthermore, we observed more cavitation bubbles and more uniform bubble density distribution inside the holes in salt solution than in water. The cavitation bubbles at their highest expansion can displace up to 20% of the liquid from the holes. Thus, the bubbles could act as mixers and pumps rapidly exchanging the liquid in the holes. This might open up micromixing and micropump application for microfluidics processes (e.g. lab-on-a-chip applications), however further research is needed. In particular, the flow behavior inside the holes resulting from the cavitation collapse has to be investigated in details.

Additionally, in this work, the behavior of cavitation bubbles inside the holes was discussed in terms of applied ultrasound power. Reducing the ultrasound power leads to cavitation bubble shift to the inner end of the blind holes or to the center of the through holes. A possible reason could be shielding of the sound wave at high ultrasound power due to the presence of cavitation bubbles at the hole entrances. Although the results may be specific to the investigated system, they are supposed to provide a way to control the cavitation distribution inside more complicated structures as well. This might induce benefit for any potential wet surface treatment including cleaning processes and surface modification. The effect of the ultrasound frequency and other substrates (e.g. glass, flexible materials) on the cavitation bubble dynamics might point out a direction of further investigations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Atotech Deutschland GmbH for financial support and the members of the mechanical workshop at DPI for valuable support. R. M. and C. C. acknowledge financial support by the Austrian Federal Ministry of Economy, Family and Youth and the National Foundation for Research, Technology and Development.
